claims_to_verify: Current pricing tiers and credit systems for Midjourney, Adobe Firefly, DALL-E 3 (via ChatGPT and API), Stable Diffusion (SDXL and SD3), Flux.1, and Ideogram as of the article publish date
Which tools offer commercial licensing and under what plan tiers
Whether Midjourney allows API access or is still Discord/web-only
Flux.1 model variants (Pro, Dev, Schnell) and their actual availability through third-party platforms vs. direct access
Adobe Firefly's Content Credentials and IP-safe training claims, and any independent verification of those claims
DALL-E 3 native resolution and upscaling options

Which tools support inpainting, outpainting, and image-to-image workflows natively
Ideogram's current typography and photorealism capabilities relative to stated version (Ideogram 2.0 or later)

brief.research_plan: Prioritize official documentation and pricing pages for each tool for accuracy on cost and licensing. Look for independent technical evaluations and benchmark comparisons from AI art and photography communities (e.g., community posts on Reddit r/StableDiffusion, Midjourney Discord feedback threads, or creator YouTube channels that do side-by-side prompt tests). Avoid relying on the tools' own marketing pages for capability claims. For commercial licensing, go directly to terms of service pages. For real-world output quality, find creator forums or published prompt comparisons with visible outputs across identical or near-identical prompts. Note publication dates carefully since model versions update frequently.

brief.target_reader: Independent musicians and music producers who are curious about AI tools but wary of overpromised results. Includes singer-songwriters, beatmakers, film/game composers, and bedroom producers at intermediate skill level who already use DAWs and want to know where AI genuinely fits into their workflow.
brief.reader_outcome: The reader can identify which AI music tools are relevant to their specific production stage (composition, arrangement, mixing, mastering, vocal processing, licensing), evaluate tradeoffs between tools, and take immediate next steps to try at least one in their existing workflow.

Check US Copyright Office official statements and any 2024-2025 rulings on AI-generated music ownership. Look for hands-on reviews from music production communities such as Reddit r/WeAreTheMusicMakers, Gearspace, and music technology press (MusicRadar, Sound On Sound, Attack Magazine) for real-world quality assessments rather than vendor claims. Verify DAW-native AI features from official release notes or developer blogs, not press speculation. For licensing and copyright, cross-reference Electronic Frontier Foundation commentary and any recent legal cases involving AI music tools.

sources.title: Suno Pricing Page
claims_supported: Suno has three tiers: Free ($0, 50 credits/day, ~10 songs/day), Pro ($10/month or $8/month annually, 2,500 credits/month), and Premier ($30/month or $24/month annually, 10,000 credits/month). Commercial use limitations exist — see terms of service.
Purchased top-up credits require an active subscription to use.
Credits included in subscriptions do not carry over between billing periods.

sources.title: Suno Commercial Use 2026: Rights and Ownership Guide
sources.publisher: TechJack Solutions (independent analysis citing Suno ToS and help documentation)
claims_supported: Free-tier songs are non-commercial only; Suno retains ownership of free-plan songs.
Pro and Premier plans include commercial licensing; commercial rights are identical across both paid tiers.
Suno's ToS states: 'due to the nature of machine learning, Suno makes no representation or warranty to you that any copyright will vest in any Output.'
Suno grants users a broad, worldwide, perpetual, irrevocable, sublicensable license back to submitted content for model training.
Commercial rights attach only to songs generated while a paid subscription is active; retroactive rights for free-plan songs are not granted.
In November 2025, Suno reached a $500 million settlement with Warner Music Group and announced a licensing partnership. Sony had not settled as of March 2026.
RIAA sued Suno and Udio in June 2024, seeking up to $150,000 in statutory damages per work.
Suno is building new AI models trained on licensed music; current v5.x models are expected to be deprecated in 2026.

sources.title: Udio Pricing Page (verified via direct browser review, June 15, 2026)
claims_supported: Udio has three tiers: Free ($0, 100 credits/month with 10/day cap), Standard ($10/month or $8/month annually, 2,400 credits), and Pro ($30/month or $24/month annually, 6,000 credits).
Add-on credit packs available: 100 credits for $3, 1,000 credits for $25; purchased credits never expire.
Commercial rights on Udio require a paid Standard or Pro plan; free tier is restricted to non-commercial use.

Following October 2025 UMG settlement and November 2025 WMG settlement, Udio moved toward a 'walled garden' model where users stream rather than freely download outputs — reshaping what a subscription buys.

sources.title: AIVA Terms of Service / License Agreement
claims_supported: AIVA grants one of three license types upon download depending on the user's plan: Non-Commercial License (Free tier), Limited Commercial License (Standard tier), or Full Copyright (Pro tier).
Free plan: non-commercial use only, attribution to AIVA required, copyright owned by AIVA.
Standard plan: social media monetization allowed (YouTube, Twitch, TikTok, Instagram), attribution required.
Pro plan: full copyright ownership transferred to user, unlimited commercial use, no attribution required.
Enterprise: custom licensing available.
Free plan allows up to 3 downloads/month, MP3 and MIDI formats; Pro plan allows up to 300 downloads/month including WAV and stems.
AIVA is registered as a composer with SACEM (French/Luxembourg authors' rights society).

sources.title: SOUNDRAW Music Licensing & Legal Use FAQ
claims_supported: SOUNDRAW retains neighboring rights (rights to sound recordings) for all AI-generated music. Users are granted a royalty-free license to use tracks in personal and commercial projects.
Users own any creative elements they add to the music (e.g., added vocals, changed composition), but the copyright of the full AI-generated song remains with SOUNDRAW.
Users cannot register SOUNDRAW music with YouTube Content ID or claim it as their own copyrighted material.
SOUNDRAW's AI is trained exclusively on music produced in-house by SOUNDRAW's professional music producers — no scraped or third-party copyrighted works used.
All SOUNDRAW plans (Creator or Artist) allow content monetization.
Under the Artist Plan, users who add original vocals or instruments own the resulting song fully and keep 100% of recording royalties; they cannot sell or distribute SOUNDRAW beats alone.

sources.title: iZotope Ozone 11 – Why Upgrade / Official Product Page (Ozone 12 Advanced)
claims_supported: Ozone is available as a perpetual standalone purchase (Elements ~$49, Standard ~$199, Advanced ~$399 for Ozone 11; Standard loyalty pricing from $129 for Ozone 12) AND as part of subscriptions/bundles (Music Production Suite 6 at $599, Mix & Master Bundle Advanced at $499, and via Native Instruments subscription).
Ozone is NOT subscription-only; it can be purchased as a one-time standalone plugin in Elements, Standard, or Advanced tiers.
All Ozone tiers include the AI Master Assistant feature; Elements is the most AI-automated; Standard and Advanced provide increasing manual control.
Ozone 12 (current version as of 2025–2026) includes Stem EQ, Bass Control, Unlimiter, and AI Master Assistant.
Ozone 11 is also available via Splice plugin rental at $9.99/month (Standard) or $19.99/month (Advanced).
Ozone runs as a standalone app and as AAX, AU, and VST3 plugins across Logic Pro, Ableton Live, FL Studio, Pro Tools, and other major DAWs.

sources.title: Moises App Official Website + App Store / Google Play listings
claims_supported: Moises features include: AI stem separation (vocals, drums, bass, guitar, piano, strings), chord detection/finder, pitch & tempo change, speed changer, lyric transcription, smart metronome, and AI Studio (desktop).
Free plan covers: vocals/drums/bass stem separation, chord detector, metronome, pitch shifting, BPM detection, and setlists.
Premium plan adds: unlimited AI audio separation, advanced instrument separation (lead/rhythm guitar, acoustic/electric guitar, main/background vocals), fully unlocked practice tools.

AI Studio (desktop) adds: generate instrument stems/arrangements from chord progressions, voice conversion using licensed AI voice models (royalty-free, commercially licensed), and integrated mixing/mastering.
Moises does NOT train on user-uploaded music; it trains only on licensed materials.
Moises has 70 million users and was a 2024 iPad App of the Year winner and 2025 Microsoft Store Awards winner.
AI backing track creation is achieved via stem separation (muting stems) rather than a dedicated 'AI backing track generator' — the AI Studio adds arrangement generation from chord progressions.

sources.title: US Copyright Office, Copyright and Artificial Intelligence Part 2: Copyrightability (January 29, 2025) — as analyzed by Jones Day and Congress.gov CRS
sources.publisher: U.S. Copyright Office / Jones Day / Congress.gov
claims_supported: The US Copyright Office published Part 2 of its Copyright and AI Report on January 29, 2025, addressing the copyrightability of AI-generated outputs.
Fully AI-generated outputs produced solely in response to a prompt, without further human creative intervention, are NOT copyrightable under current US law.
Human contributions to AI-generated outputs can be sufficient to constitute authorship, but must be analyzed on a case-by-case basis by Copyright Examiners.

In March 2025, the US Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit affirmed (Thaler v. Perlmutter) that human authorship is a bedrock requirement of copyright; the en banc rehearing petition was denied May 12, 2025.
Works created by humans with the assistance of generative AI may qualify for copyright protection depending on the nature and degree of human creative involvement.
Copyright protection, where it exists, covers only the human-authored elements — not the AI-generated portions.

sources.title: Logic Pro AI Features – MusicRadar Report + Loopcloud DAW AI Comparison 2026
claims_supported: Logic Pro is the DAW most committed to deep, native AI integration among major DAWs, with Apple building AI directly into core creative workflows.

Logic Pro's shipped AI features include: Session Players (AI-powered drums, bass, keys, and synths), Stem Splitter, Flashback Capture, AI-powered lyric tools, and Music Understanding (iPad — uses AI-based awareness to find sounds via natural language prompts).
Logic Pro also includes a Synth Player and chord progression generation from audio/MIDI recordings (shipped as of latest version covered in January 2026 report).
Ableton Live does not lead in native AI features as of 2026; it offers flexibility for third-party AI plugin integration rather than built-in AI tools.
FL Studio received an agentic AI chatbot feature for in-DAW control (reported by MusicRadar, 2026); FL Studio Web (browser-based) launched in late 2025 beta.
Logic Pro (and its apps) are now available as part of Apple's Creator Studio subscription at $12.99/month or $129/year, in addition to individual purchase.

sources.title: Best AI Mastering Plugins 2026 — LANDR coverage
claims_supported: LANDR is a cloud-only AI mastering service (not an in-DAW plugin); pricing starts from approximately $9–$11.99/month, with higher tiers unlocking unlimited masters, distribution credits, and 24-bit WAV exports.
Free LANDR preview masters are watermarked at high-quality download tier.
LANDR's mastering quality is noted as consistent across genres but can feel generic on records needing a more opinionated finish — a recurring independent observation.
LANDR is mastering-only and assumes a finished mix; it will not correct mix problems, only master them.
LANDR also offers ReHance (vocal enhancement) and Layers (instrumental generation) as part of its broader platform.

unverifiable_claims: Specific current pricing for Boomy, Splice AI (as a standalone AI music product — Splice is primarily a sample marketplace/plugin rental service; 'Splice AI' as a distinct AI music generation product could not be verified as a discrete offering), Melodyne, and ElevenLabs Music — these were not researched within the available search budget and must be independently verified before publication.

ElevenLabs Music as a named public product: ElevenLabs is primarily a voice/TTS platform; whether they have launched a dedicated 'Music' product or voice-to-instrument AI tool in public availability could not be confirmed and should be verified directly at elevenlabs.io before inclusion.
Specific current pricing for Landr beyond the approximate $9–$11.99/month starting range — exact tier names and prices for 2025/2026 require verification at landr.com/pricing.
Specific current pricing for Melodyne — Celemony's Melodyne pricing tiers (Essential, Assistant, Editor, Studio) and exact current prices were not researched in this session; verify at celemony.com.

Moises 'AI backing tracks' as a named feature: Moises enables backing-track-style use via stem muting and AI Studio arrangement generation from chord progressions, but does not market a discrete feature explicitly called 'AI backing tracks' — the brief's framing should be softened to reflect how the feature actually works.

AIVA exact monthly/annual pricing in USD — sources cited prices in EUR (€11/month starting) and noted VAT complexity; exact USD prices should be confirmed directly at aiva.ai/pricing before publication.

Udio commercial licensing clarity post-UMG settlement: multiple sources flag that commercial-use rights for Udio remain somewhat ambiguous following the October 2025 UMG settlement and resulting 'walled garden' model change. The claim that paid Udio plans clearly include commercial rights should be softened with a note to verify current ToS at udio.com.

Suno free tier model access: some sources indicate the free plan is locked to an older model (v4.5-all) while paid plans access v5.5 — the exact model available on the free tier at publication time should be verified at suno.com/pricing as this changes frequently.

article.title: AI Tools for Musicians: A Practical Guide to What Actually Works
alternative_titles: The Working Musician's Guide to AI Music Tools
AI in Music Production: Which Tools Fit Which Workflows
article.dek: A stage-by-stage breakdown of AI tools for composition, mixing, mastering, vocals, and more. With honest notes on licensing, copyright, and where each tool falls short.
article.meta_description: Independent musicians and producers: here's where AI tools genuinely help in your workflow, which tools to evaluate at each production stage, and what the licensing and copyright fine print actually says.
article.quick_answer: AI music tools are most reliable for mastering (iZotope Ozone, LANDR), stem separation (Moises), and DAW-native arrangement assistance (Logic Pro Session Players). Full-song generation tools like Suno and Udio are useful for demos and reference tracks, but commercial rights and copyright ownership involve real caveats worth reading before you publish.
article.introduction:
AI is shipping inside tools you may already own, and it is also arriving as standalone services with pricing tiers, terms of service, and copyright implications that vary widely. The noise-to-signal ratio in coverage of AI music tools is high, so this guide skips the hype and focuses on specific production stages: what the tools actually do, where they help intermediate producers, and where they create new problems.

This guide covers composition and generation, arrangement, mixing, mastering, vocal processing, stem separation, and licensing. Pricing and terms cited here were reviewed against official documentation as of mid-2026 but change frequently. Always verify directly at each platform before committing.

sections.heading: What to Expect From AI in Music Production (and What to Ignore)
sections.body_markdown:
AI tools in music currently do a few things well: they automate time-consuming but rule-based tasks (like loudness normalization or stem separation), they generate usable raw material quickly (chord progressions, arrangement variations, reference masters), and they lower the floor for tasks that previously required either expensive plugins or specialist knowledge.

What they do not reliably do: replace taste, make aesthetic decisions for you, or produce finished, release-ready output without human intervention. Tools marketed as "one click to release" almost always produce generic results that experienced listeners notice.

A useful mental model: treat AI tools as fast assistants that need direction, not replacements for your production judgment. The more specific the task and the more human editing you apply afterward, the better the output.

sections.heading: AI for Composition and Full-Song Generation
sections.body_markdown:
Full-song AI generators let you create complete tracks from text prompts or style inputs. They are genuinely useful for reference tracks, lo-fi demos, and sync music sketches. They are less useful as finished releases, partly for quality reasons and partly for copyright reasons (see the licensing section below).

**Suno** offers a free tier (50 credits/day, approximately 10 songs) with non-commercial restrictions. Pro ($10/month) and Premier ($30/month) plans add commercial rights and more credits. Critically, Suno's own terms state it makes no representation that copyright will vest in any output, and free-plan songs are owned by Suno, not you. Commercial rights attach only to songs generated under an active paid subscription.

**Udio** has a similar structure: Free (100 credits/month, with a 10 credits/day cap, non-commercial), Standard ($10/month), and Pro ($30/month). Following reported settlements with UMG and WMG in late 2025, Udio has reportedly moved toward a model where users stream rather than freely download outputs. Verify current download terms at udio.com before using output commercially.

**AIVA** takes a different approach, targeting composers who want melodic and harmonic frameworks rather than full song audio. Its free tier allows up to 3 downloads/month (MP3 and MIDI), non-commercial only, copyright owned by AIVA. The Standard plan allows social media monetization with attribution. The Pro plan transfers full copyright to the user with no attribution required. AIVA is registered as a composer with SACEM (the French/Luxembourg authors' rights society), a notable distinction among AI music tools. However, this registration does not resolve questions about copyright ownership of outputs under US law, where human authorship requirements still apply.

For composers working in MIDI-based workflows, AIVA's MIDI export makes it a more integrable tool than Suno or Udio, which output audio only.

tools.name: Suno
tools.description: Text-to-song AI generator. Produces full audio tracks including vocals from prompts.
strengths: Fast full-song output from text prompts
Useful for demos and reference tracks
Generous free tier for non-commercial exploration
limitations: No copyright guarantee in output even on paid plans
Commercial rights require active paid subscription
Audio-only output, no MIDI or stems on standard plans
Ongoing litigation (RIAA) introduces long-term rights uncertainty

tools.name: Udio
tools.description: Text-to-song generator with style and genre controls.
strengths: Credit packs (purchased separately) never expire
Competitive pricing at parity with Suno
limitations: Post-UMG/WMG settlement, download model changed to streaming-first
Commercial rights clarity on paid tiers is uncertain as of mid-2026
Free tier limited to 10 credits/day

tools.name: AIVA
tools.description: AI composition tool focused on melodic and harmonic frameworks, targeting composers and film/game scoring.
strengths: MIDI export integrates into DAW workflows
Clear, tiered copyright transfer (full ownership on Pro plan)
More defined licensing structure than audio-only generators
limitations: Free tier is non-commercial, copyright owned by AIVA
Standard plan still requires attribution
Less suited to producers who work primarily in audio

sections.heading: AI for Arrangement and DAW-Native Features
sections.body_markdown:
If you use Logic Pro, it currently offers the most integrated native AI features of any major DAW. Shipped features include Session Players (AI-driven drums, bass, keys, and synths that respond to your track), Stem Splitter, Flashback Capture, and chord progression generation from audio or MIDI recordings. A Synth Player and Music Understanding (natural language sound search on iPad) have also shipped. These tools live inside your existing workflow without a separate subscription.

FL Studio reportedly received an agentic AI chatbot for in-DAW control, and FL Studio Web (browser-based) reportedly launched in late 2025 beta, according to independent reporting. Verify current feature availability at image-line.com. Ableton Live does not lead in native AI features as of mid-2026; its strength remains third-party plugin integration rather than built-in AI tools.

For producers not on Logic Pro, **AIVA's** MIDI output or third-party chord/arrangement tools are the most DAW-agnostic options for arrangement assistance. **Moises AI Studio** (desktop) also allows generating instrument stems and arrangements from chord progressions, though it is more of a practice and arrangement sketching tool than a deep compositional environment.

sections.heading: AI Mixing and Mastering: Where It Helps and Where It Falls Short
sections.body_markdown:
This is the production stage where AI tools deliver the most consistent, immediately useful results for independent producers. The tasks (loudness targeting, EQ matching, limiting) are rule-based enough that AI handles them well, and the feedback loop (does it sound good?) is fast.

**iZotope Ozone** is available as a one-time perpetual purchase across Elements, Standard, and Advanced tiers (Ozone 11 list prices were approximately $49/$199/$399; Ozone 12 pricing differs. Verify current pricing at izotope.com before purchasing). Subscription bundles and Splice plugin rental are also available. It is not subscription-only. All tiers include the AI Master Assistant, which analyzes your track and suggests a starting mastering chain. Elements is the most automated; Standard and Advanced add increasing manual control. Ozone 12 added Stem EQ, Bass Control, and Unlimiter. It runs as a standalone app and as AAX, AU, and VST3 across all major DAWs.

The AI Master Assistant is genuinely useful as a starting point, particularly if you are not a dedicated mastering engineer. The limitation is that it cannot diagnose mix problems before they reach the mastering stage. If your mix has phase issues, muddiness, or weak low-end translation, Ozone will master those problems in, not out.

**LANDR** is primarily a cloud-based mastering service with pricing starting around $9 to $11.99 per month. While it is not a traditional in-DAW plugin, LANDR does offer a DAW plugin integration on some plans. Verify current integration options at landr.com before assuming a cloud-only workflow. Independent reviewers note that LANDR produces consistent results across genres but can feel generic on records that need an opinionated finish. It is mastering-focused and assumes a finished mix. Free preview masters are watermarked at high-quality download tier. LANDR also offers ReHance (vocal enhancement) and Layers (instrumental generation) as part of its broader platform.

tools.name: iZotope Ozone
tools.description: AI-assisted mastering plugin and standalone app. Available as perpetual purchase or subscription/rental.
strengths: AI Master Assistant included in all tiers, including the lower-cost Elements
One-time purchase option available, not subscription-only
Runs natively inside Logic, Ableton, FL Studio, Pro Tools, and more
Ozone 12 adds Stem EQ for mastering-stage tonal adjustments per stem
limitations: Cannot fix mix problems, only master them
Advanced tier is expensive at ~$399; free trials are time-limited
AI suggestions are a starting point, not a finished master

tools.name: LANDR
tools.description: Cloud-only AI mastering service with distribution and vocal enhancement add-ons.
strengths: Fast turnaround with no plugin installation
Consistent across genres for straightforward mixes
Includes distribution credits on higher tiers
limitations: Cloud-only, no in-DAW integration
Independent reviewers describe output as generic on records needing character
Free previews are watermarked
Exact current tier pricing should be verified at landr.com/pricing

sections.heading: AI Vocal Tools: Pitch Correction and Stem Separation
sections.body_markdown:
For pitch correction, Melodyne is among the most widely used tools in professional contexts, particularly for polyphonic correction. Current pricing tiers (Essential, Assistant, Editor, Studio) and exact USD prices were not confirmed for this guide. Verify current pricing at celemony.com before purchasing.

For stem separation, **Moises** is the most feature-complete dedicated tool currently available. The free plan covers vocal, drum, and bass separation, chord detection, pitch shifting, and BPM detection. The Premium plan adds unlimited separation and advanced instrument isolation (lead vs. rhythm guitar, main vs. background vocals, acoustic vs. electric guitar). Moises does not train on user-uploaded audio; it trains only on licensed materials.

An important clarification: Moises does not offer a feature explicitly called "AI backing tracks." What it does is allow you to mute stems from a separated track, which achieves a similar result. The desktop AI Studio adds arrangement generation from chord progressions and voice conversion using commercially licensed AI voice models.

On voice cloning tools: this is an ethically and legally contested area. Tools exist that can clone a voice from a short sample, and the music industry has seen several high-profile unauthorized cases. Before using any voice conversion or cloning tool, verify that you have explicit permission or own the voice being used. Moises's voice conversion feature uses pre-cleared, royalty-free licensed models specifically to avoid this issue.

tools.name: Moises
tools.description: AI stem separation, chord detection, pitch/tempo tools, and AI Studio for arrangement and voice conversion.
strengths: Advanced instrument separation on Premium (lead/rhythm guitar, main/background vocals)
Does not train on user-uploaded audio
AI Studio voice conversion uses commercially licensed, royalty-free models
Cross-platform: iOS, Android, and desktop
limitations: No dedicated "AI backing track" generator; backing track use requires stem muting workflow
Free tier limits instrument separation to vocals, drums, and bass only
AI Studio arrangement generation is more useful for sketching than finished production

sections.heading: AI for Samples and Sound Design
sections.body_markdown:
**Soundraw** generates royalty-free music that can be customized and used in video, social content, or commercial projects. Soundraw retains neighboring rights (sound recording rights) to all AI-generated output, but grants users a royalty-free license for personal and commercial use. Users cannot register Soundraw music with YouTube Content ID or claim it as their own copyrighted material.

The Artist Plan creates an important distinction: if you add your own original vocals or instrumentation, you own the resulting song fully and keep 100% of recording royalties. You cannot, however, sell or distribute Soundraw beats alone without your own creative additions. Soundraw's training data is produced in-house by its own team, not scraped from third-party sources.

For sample-based producers, Splice's sample marketplace remains a primary destination for cleared loops and one-shots. However, a distinct product called "Splice AI" as a standalone AI music generation tool could not be confirmed as a current discrete offering at time of research. Verify any Splice AI product claims directly at splice.com.

tools.name: Soundraw
tools.description: AI music generator with royalty-free licensing for commercial use in video and content projects.
strengths: Trained on in-house produced music, not scraped content
Artist Plan allows full ownership if you add original contributions
Commercial use allowed on all plans
limitations: You cannot register Soundraw tracks with YouTube Content ID
Copyright of the AI-generated layer stays with Soundraw
Not suited for use as standalone beats without creative additions if you want full ownership

sections.heading: Licensing and Copyright: What the Law Currently Says
sections.body_markdown:
The US Copyright Office published Part 2 of its Copyright and AI Report on January 29, 2025. The core finding: fully AI-generated outputs produced solely in response to a prompt, without further human creative intervention, are not copyrightable under current US law. In March 2025, the US Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit affirmed this in Thaler v. Perlmutter, and an en banc rehearing petition was denied on May 12, 2025. Human authorship remains a legal requirement for copyright protection.

Where humans make meaningful creative choices in combination with AI tools, copyright protection may apply, but only to the human-authored elements. This means: if you generate a full song by typing a prompt into Suno and publish it, you likely have no copyright in that output. If you use AI-generated MIDI as a starting point, substantially rearrange it, add original instrumentation, and record new performances on top, the human-authored elements may be protectable.

Practical implications for independent musicians:

- Suno's terms explicitly state it makes no warranty that copyright will vest in any output.
- Free-tier Suno songs are owned by Suno, not the user.
- Udio's commercial rights clarity has been complicated by its post-settlement model changes.
- AIVA's Pro plan claims full copyright transfer to the user. However, under US Copyright Office guidance, if the output lacks sufficient human creative input, it may not be copyrightable at all — meaning there is no copyright to transfer, regardless of what AIVA's terms state. Users planning to assert copyright in AIVA outputs should ensure substantial human creative intervention is documented.
- SOUNDRAW tracks cannot be registered with YouTube Content ID regardless of plan.

Major record labels (including Sony Music, Universal Music Group, and Warner Music Group, coordinated by the RIAA) sued both Suno and Udio in June 2024, seeking up to $150,000 in statutory damages per work. Settlement and licensing developments have been reported since, but the details of which labels have settled with which AI companies remain difficult to verify from primary sources as of mid-2026. These are ongoing legal developments worth monitoring if you plan to build a commercial catalog using these tools. Verify current status through primary legal filings or major press reporting before drawing conclusions about rights exposure.

sections.heading: How to Evaluate Any AI Music Tool Before Committing
sections.body_markdown:
Use these criteria before subscribing to or building a workflow around any AI music tool:

**Copyright and ownership.** Read the terms of service directly, not a summary. Specifically: who owns the output? Does commercial licensing require an active subscription? Does the platform claim any training rights over your submissions?

**Output format.** Does the tool export MIDI, stems, or audio only? MIDI and stem export significantly increase a tool's usefulness inside a DAW-based workflow.

**Integration.** Does it run as a plugin in your DAW, as a standalone app, or cloud-only? Cloud-only tools require a live internet connection and carry availability risk.

**Free tier limitations.** Understand exactly what the free tier produces. Is output watermarked? Is it limited to older model versions? Are exports restricted by format or resolution?

**Reputation in production communities.** Check r/WeAreTheMusicMakers, Gearspace, and music tech press (Sound On Sound, MusicRadar, Attack Magazine) for user reports on output quality. Vendor demos are optimized; community posts are not.

**Pricing stability.** Several tools in this space have changed pricing tiers and terms significantly in 2024 to 2026. Note when you last verified pricing and set a reminder to check it before renewing.

sections.heading: Suggested Workflows by Use Case
sections.body_markdown:
**Singer-songwriter using a DAW for demos.** Use Logic Pro's Session Players to build an arrangement skeleton around your recorded performance. Export stems, use Moises for separation if you need to work with reference tracks. Master with Ozone Elements as a starting point before sending to a professional mastering engineer if releasing commercially.

**Beatmaker or producer making instrumental releases.** Generate harmonic or melodic ideas with AIVA (Pro plan for copyright transfer, noting the caveats above) and import MIDI into your DAW. Use Ozone Standard or Advanced for mastering. Avoid publishing Suno or Udio audio output as a finished commercial release given current copyright uncertainty.

**Film or game composer under deadline.** AIVA's MIDI output is more useful here than audio-only generators because it integrates directly into a scoring workflow. Use Logic Pro's chord progression tools or Session Players for quick arrangement sketches. SOUNDRAW is useful for client demos or placeholder temp tracks where full ownership is not required.

**Content creator needing background music.** Soundraw's plans allow royalty-free commercial use, but users cannot register Soundraw tracks with YouTube Content ID. If another party registers the music, a Content ID claim could still occur. If you add original contributions under the Artist Plan, you gain full ownership of the resulting recording. Verify current plan names and Content ID terms at soundraw.io.

**Producer wanting to practice against stems.** Moises free tier covers the core use case: separate stems from reference tracks, mute what you want to play over, use chord detection to understand the harmony. Premium unlocks more precise instrument separation if you need it.

key_takeaways: AI mastering tools (iZotope Ozone, LANDR) are the highest-reliability entry point: clear use case, fast feedback loop, and Ozone is available as a one-time purchase.

Full-song generators (Suno, Udio) are useful for demos and reference tracks but involve real copyright uncertainty: free-tier output is non-commercial, and fully AI-generated audio is not copyrightable under current US law.
AIVA and Soundraw have more defined licensing structures than audio-only generators, but ownership caveats still apply and should be read in the terms of service directly.
Logic Pro currently offers the deepest native AI integration of any major DAW with Session Players, Stem Splitter, and chord tools shipping in the current version.

Before using any AI-generated audio commercially, read the platform's terms of service, understand the US Copyright Office's 2025 guidance on AI outputs, and note that ongoing RIAA litigation affects Suno and Udio specifically.
article.conclusion:
AI tools are most useful to working musicians when applied to specific, bounded tasks inside an existing workflow. Mastering assistance, stem separation, arrangement sketching, and reference track generation are all areas where the tools are good enough to save real time without requiring you to overhaul how you work.

The full-song generation space is moving fast but carries legal and creative risks that are easy to underestimate. The copyright situation is not vague by accident: it reflects genuinely unresolved questions about machine authorship that courts and regulators are still working through. Treat AI-generated audio as raw material that requires human creative intervention, both for better output and for any claim to ownership of the result.

Start with one tool that addresses a specific bottleneck in your current workflow. Verify its terms directly, try the free tier, and evaluate whether the output actually saves you time or improves what you make. That is a more reliable test than any review, including this one.
article.methodology_disclosure: This guide was researched and synthesized using official pricing pages, terms of service, and licensing documentation from Suno, Udio, AIVA, Soundraw, iZotope, and Moises, supplemented by independent reporting from MusicRadar, MixingGPT, and an independent commercial use analysis of Suno's ToS. Copyright claims trace to the US Copyright Office's January 2025 AI Copyrightability Report and the D.C. Circuit's March 2025 ruling in Thaler v. Perlmutter, as analyzed by Jones Day. Pricing and terms were verified as of mid-2026 but are subject to change; readers should verify directly at each platform before making purchasing decisions. Claims about tools for which verified pricing or feature data was unavailable (Boomy, Splice AI as a discrete product, Melodyne current pricing, ElevenLabs Music as a product) were omitted or explicitly flagged as unverified rather than estimated. No hands-on product testing was conducted by dailyblip for this guide. Community reputation observations are editorial judgments drawn from independent review sources and are identified as such.

issues.original_text: Suno reached a $500 million settlement with Warner Music Group in November 2025 and announced a licensing partnership. Sony had not settled as of March 2026.
issues.issue: The RIAA lawsuit against Suno was filed by major labels including Sony Music, Universal Music Group, and Warner Music Group collectively — not WMG alone. The source cited (TechJack Solutions, an independent third-party site) is not a primary source for settlement details, and a $500 million figure for a Suno-WMG-only settlement is not verifiable from the sources provided. The source notes a WMG settlement but this figure and framing needs verification. Additionally, the intro states 'Sony had not settled as of March 2026' but the RIAA suit included UMG, WMG, and Sony — implying only Sony remains is potentially misleading if UMG also settled separately or not at all. The Udio source references UMG and WMG settlements but not Suno's. Cross-referencing the two sources creates confusion about which label settled with which company.
issues.recommended_replacement: Verify settlement details directly from primary legal filings or major press reporting before publishing. At minimum, clarify which labels are party to which settlements with which AI company, and note the $500 million figure is sourced from a single independent third-party site rather than a primary source. Consider flagging this as unverified pending direct confirmation.

issues.original_text: The RIAA sued both Suno and Udio in June 2024 seeking up to $150,000 in statutory damages per work.
issues.issue: The RIAA itself did not file the lawsuits — the suits were filed by major record labels (Sony Music Entertainment, Universal Music Group Recordings, Warner Records, etc.) as plaintiffs. The RIAA supported and coordinated the litigation but is a trade association, not the legal plaintiff. Attributing the suit to 'the RIAA' is a common but technically inaccurate shorthand that could mislead readers about the legal parties involved.
issues.recommended_replacement: Major record labels (including Sony Music, Universal Music Group, and Warner Music Group, coordinated by the RIAA) sued both Suno and Udio in June 2024, seeking up to $150,000 in statutory damages per work.

issues.original_text: Udio has a similar structure: Free (100 credits/month, non-commercial), Standard ($10/month), and Pro ($30/month). Following settlements with UMG and WMG in late 2025, Udio moved toward a model where users stream rather than freely download outputs.
issues.issue: The Udio source lists the free tier as having a 10 credits/day cap in addition to the 100 credits/month ceiling. The article omits the daily cap, which is a material limitation for users evaluating the free tier. Also, the claim that Udio 'moved toward a model where users stream rather than freely download outputs' is sourced from a single pricing page review, not from Udio's official terms or a primary press release — this is a significant policy claim that warrants stronger sourcing or explicit uncertainty flagging.
issues.recommended_replacement: Udio has a similar structure: Free (100 credits/month, with a 10 credits/day cap, non-commercial), Standard ($10/month), and Pro ($30/month). Following reported settlements with UMG and WMG in late 2025, Udio has reportedly moved toward a model where users stream rather than freely download outputs — verify current download terms at udio.com before using output commercially.

issues.original_text: iZotope Ozone is available as a one-time perpetual purchase in Elements (~$49), Standard (~$199), and Advanced (~$399) tiers
issues.issue: The source notes these as Ozone 11 prices and also references 'Standard loyalty pricing from $129 for Ozone 12.' The article presents the ~$49/$199/$399 figures without specifying they are Ozone 11 prices and may not reflect current Ozone 12 pricing. Presenting approximate prices without version-specific context could mislead readers into expecting outdated pricing for the current version.
issues.recommended_replacement: iZotope Ozone is available as a one-time perpetual purchase across Elements, Standard, and Advanced tiers (Ozone 11 list prices were approximately $49/$199/$399; Ozone 12 pricing differs — verify current pricing at izotope.com before purchasing).

issues.original_text: LANDR is a cloud-only mastering service (not an in-DAW plugin) with pricing starting around $9 to $11.99 per month.
issues.issue: LANDR does offer a desktop app/plugin integration (LANDR Studio plugin) that allows in-DAW mastering chain insertion, which contradicts the 'not an in-DAW plugin' claim. This is a capability distinction that meaningfully affects how producers evaluate the tool.
issues.recommended_replacement: LANDR is primarily a cloud-based mastering service with pricing starting around $9 to $11.99 per month. While it is not a traditional in-DAW plugin, LANDR does offer a DAW plugin integration on some plans — verify current integration options at landr.com before assuming cloud-only workflow.

issues.original_text: AIVA is registered as a composer with SACEM, which makes its licensing framework more clearly defined than many competitors.
issues.issue: AIVA's registration with SACEM as a 'composer' is an unusual and disputed status — SACEM is a French authors' rights society and this registration has been noted as a novelty/marketing claim rather than a straightforward legal fact with clear implications. Presenting it as making AIVA's licensing 'more clearly defined' is an editorial conclusion that overstates what SACEM registration means for users' copyright position, especially given US Copyright Office guidance that AI outputs require human authorship for protection.
issues.recommended_replacement: AIVA is registered as a composer with SACEM (the French/Luxembourg authors' rights society), a notable distinction among AI music tools. However, this registration does not resolve questions about copyright ownership of outputs under US law, where human authorship requirements still apply.

issues.original_text: AIVA's Pro plan claims full copyright transfer to the user, but given US Copyright Office guidance, the enforceability of that transfer depends on how much human creative input is involved in the output.
issues.issue: This is directionally correct but imprecise in a legally important way. AIVA can contractually transfer whatever rights it holds, but if the output is not copyrightable under US law (because it lacks human authorship), there is nothing to transfer — the issue is not 'enforceability of the transfer' but whether any copyright exists in the first place to be transferred. The framing could mislead readers into thinking they have copyright protection contingent on human input level, when the threshold question is whether copyright exists at all.
issues.recommended_replacement: AIVA's Pro plan claims full copyright transfer to the user. However, under US Copyright Office guidance, if the output lacks sufficient human creative input, it may not be copyrightable at all — meaning there is no copyright to transfer, regardless of what AIVA's terms state. Users planning to assert copyright in AIVA outputs should ensure substantial human creative intervention is documented.

issues.original_text: FL Studio received an agentic AI chatbot for in-DAW control, and FL Studio Web (browser-based) launched in late 2025 beta.
issues.issue: The source for this claim is listed as MusicRadar/Loopcloud independent reporting, categorized as 'independent observation' rather than verified fact. Describing FL Studio Web as having 'launched in late 2025 beta' without a primary source (Image-Line official announcement) risks propagating unverified release timing. The article should either source this to an Image-Line announcement or flag it as reported rather than confirmed.
issues.recommended_replacement: FL Studio reportedly received an agentic AI chatbot for in-DAW control, and FL Studio Web (browser-based) reportedly launched in late 2025 beta, according to independent reporting — verify current feature availability at image-line.com.

issues.original_text: Free ($10/month) and Premier ($30/month) plans add commercial rights and more credits.
issues.issue: Typographical error: 'Free ($10/month)' should be 'Pro ($10/month)'. The free plan is $0.
issues.recommended_replacement: Pro ($10/month) and Premier ($30/month) plans add commercial rights and more credits.

issues.original_text: Soundraw's Creator Plan is a practical fit: royalty-free, commercial use included, no Content ID conflicts as long as you understand you cannot claim the track.
issues.issue: The article uses 'Creator Plan' here but the Soundraw source documentation refers to 'Creator or Artist' plans without clearly distinguishing a plan named exactly 'Creator Plan' versus 'Artist Plan' in all contexts. More importantly, the claim 'no Content ID conflicts' is subtly misleading — the actual restriction is that users cannot register Soundraw tracks with Content ID. If a third party (e.g., Soundraw itself or another party) registers the music, a conflict could still arise. The framing implies users are fully insulated from Content ID issues, which is not guaranteed.
issues.recommended_replacement: Soundraw's plans allow royalty-free commercial use, but users cannot register Soundraw tracks with YouTube Content ID — meaning if the music is registered by another party, a Content ID claim could still occur. Verify current plan names and Content ID terms at soundraw.io.

issues.original_text: For pitch correction, Melodyne remains the most widely used tool in professional contexts, particularly for polyphonic correction. Current pricing tiers (Essential, Assistant, Editor, Studio) and exact USD prices were not confirmed for this guide.
issues.issue: The claim that Melodyne 'remains the most widely used tool in professional contexts' is an editorial assertion without a cited source. While Melodyne is widely respected, Auto-Tune (Antares) is also widely used in professional contexts, particularly for vocal production. Presenting Melodyne as definitively 'the most widely used' without sourcing overstates certainty.
issues.recommended_replacement: For pitch correction, Melodyne is among the most widely used tools in professional contexts, particularly for polyphonic correction. Current pricing tiers (Essential, Assistant, Editor, Studio) and exact USD prices were not confirmed for this guide — verify at celemony.com before purchasing.

issues.original_text: Pricing and terms cited here were verified as of mid-2026 but change frequently.
issues.issue: The methodology disclosure notes 'No hands-on product testing was conducted by dailyblip for this guide,' which is appropriately disclosed there. However, the introduction says pricing 'were verified' without clarifying that verification was based on documentation review rather than direct account access or testing. While the methodology disclosure covers this, the introduction's unqualified 'verified' could imply a more rigorous verification than documentation review alone, particularly for dynamic pricing pages.
issues.recommended_replacement: Pricing and terms cited here were reviewed against official documentation as of mid-2026 but change frequently. Always verify directly at each platform before committing.

warnings: s7: The RIAA lawsuit against Suno was filed by major labels including Sony Music, Universal Music Group, and Warner Music Group collectively — not WMG alone. The source cited (TechJack Solutions, an independent third-party site) is not a primary source for settlement details, and a $500 million figure for a Suno-WMG-only settlement is not verifiable from the sources provided. The source notes a WMG settlement but this figure and framing needs verification. Additionally, the intro states 'Sony had not settled as of March 2026' but the RIAA suit included UMG, WMG, and Sony — implying only Sony remains is potentially misleading if UMG also settled separately or not at all. The Udio source references UMG and WMG settlements but not Suno's. Cross-referencing the two sources creates confusion about which label settled with which company.

s7: The RIAA itself did not file the lawsuits — the suits were filed by major record labels (Sony Music Entertainment, Universal Music Group Recordings, Warner Records, etc.) as plaintiffs. The RIAA supported and coordinated the litigation but is a trade association, not the legal plaintiff. Attributing the suit to 'the RIAA' is a common but technically inaccurate shorthand that could mislead readers about the legal parties involved.

images.prompt: A musician's production workspace photographed from a low, intimate angle at golden hour. A sleek laptop is open on a wooden studio desk, showing abstract waveform shapes and colourful soft-glow equaliser bands—no readable text or recognisable software UI. Surrounding it: a MIDI keyboard half in frame, a pair of open-back headphones resting on the desk, a small audio interface with glowing trim, and scattered handwritten notes on paper (illegible). The mood is focused and considered, not flashy. Warm amber side-lighting with cool blue fill from the screen. Shallow depth of field. Generous empty space in the upper third for headline overlay. Editorial photography style—textured, real, no CGI elements, no glowing brains, no floating holograms. 16:9 composition.
images.alt_text: A musician's laptop and MIDI keyboard on a studio desk with warm ambient lighting
images.caption: AI tools work best as fast assistants with strong human creative direction behind them.

images.prompt: Overhead flat-lay of a composer's working surface at the moment of decision-making. A MIDI keyboard sits beside an open notebook filled with hand-drawn chord diagrams and notation (too small and abstract to read). A single pair of wired earbuds snakes across the desk. A tablet rests at an angle showing abstract horizontal stem-separation bars in muted colours—no logos, no text labels. A scattered set of small sticky notes in soft yellow and green add texture. The palette is warm neutrals—cream, walnut wood, slate grey—with a single accent of deep teal from the tablet glow. Shot from directly above with soft diffused daylight. The composition leaves the right side open for text overlay. No robots, no holograms, no stock-photo hands gesturing at nothing. 4:3 aspect ratio, editorial magazine quality.
images.alt_text: Overhead view of a composer's desk with MIDI keyboard, notebook with chord sketches, and a tablet showing stem visualisations
images.caption: Choosing the right tool depends on your output format needs—MIDI and stems integrate far more deeply into a DAW workflow than audio-only generation.

submitted.idea: Musicians quick start guide to AI

brief.target_reader: Independent musicians and producers who are curious about AI tools but have little or no hands-on experience with them. Includes singer-songwriters, beatmakers, bedroom producers, and band members working without a label or large budget. Skeptical of hype, time-constrained, and primarily interested in tools that fit into real music-making workflows.
brief.reader_outcome: The reader should be able to identify which AI music tools are relevant to their specific workflow (composition, lyrics, vocals, mixing, mastering, sound design, or distribution), set up and run at least one tool in a practical session, and evaluate tradeoffs between free and paid options before committing time or money.
structure_plan: What AI Actually Does in Music Production (and What It Still Can't Do)
Step 1: Identify Where AI Fits Your Workflow
Step 2: AI for Composition and Melody Generation
Step 3: AI for Lyrics and Songwriting Assistance
Step 4: AI for Vocals (Synthesis, Harmony, and Cloning)
Step 5: AI for Mixing and Mastering
Step 6: AI for Sound Design and Sample Creation
Step 7: AI for Release, Promotion, and Admin
Evaluating Any AI Music Tool: A Practical Checklist
Legal and Ethical Considerations Musicians Need to Know Now
Recommended Starting Stack by Use Case
claims_to_verify: Current pricing and free tier limits for Suno, Udio, and similar generative music platforms
Current pricing and capabilities of iZotope Ozone for AI mastering
Whether LANDR's AI mastering free tier still exists and what its export restrictions are
Pricing and feature scope of Melodyne's AI-assisted pitch editing versus manual mode
Whether ElevenLabs and similar voice synthesis platforms allow music/vocal use under their current terms of service
Legal status of AI voice cloning of real artists under current US and EU law or platform policy
Whether Suno and Udio allow commercial use of generated tracks under their current license terms
Training data disclosure status for major AI music tools (Suno, Udio, Adobe Project Music GenAI Control)
Whether Adobe's generative audio tools are commercially available or still in beta
Current capabilities and pricing of Splice AI tools for sample generation
Whether Meta's MusicGen or other open-source models are practically usable without coding knowledge
Distrokid, TuneCore, or similar distributor policies on distributing AI-generated music
brief.research_plan: Prioritize official pricing and terms-of-service pages for each tool over third-party summaries, which go stale quickly. Seek out musician forums (Reddit r/WeAreTheMusicMakers, Gearslutz/Gearspace, Facebook producer groups) for real user reports on output quality and workflow friction rather than relying on company demos. For legal claims, cite primary sources: RIAA statements, US Copyright Office rulings or guidance documents, and EU AI Act text where relevant. For open-source tools, check GitHub repo activity and documentation dates to confirm whether projects are actively maintained. Avoid tool comparison articles from sites with affiliate relationships unless the methodology is disclosed and reproducible.

sources.title: Suno Pricing (Official)
claims_supported: Suno Free plan: 50 credits/day (~10 songs), non-commercial use only, access to v4.5-all model
Suno Pro plan: $10/month or $8/month annually, 2,500 credits/month, commercial use rights for songs made while subscribed, access to v5.5
Suno Premier plan: $30/month or $24/month annually, 10,000 credits/month, Suno Studio, advanced stem separation
Credits in subscriptions do not roll over; purchased top-up credits do not expire but require an active subscription
Commercial rights attach only to songs generated while the subscription is active

sources.title: Suno Commercial Use: Rights, Ownership, and the Copyright Caveat
claims_supported: Suno grants paid subscribers commercial rights to stream, distribute, use in podcasts/videos/commercial projects, and sell; Suno takes 0% of royalties
Free-tier music is strictly non-commercial; Suno retains ownership of free-plan songs
Upgrading to paid does not retroactively grant commercial rights to free-tier songs
Suno's own documentation states fully AI-generated music 'would not qualify for copyright protection because a human did not write the lyrics or the music'

Warner Music Group settled with Suno in November 2025, triggering planned download restrictions: free-tier users will lose download ability (stream/share only); paid users will have monthly download caps
Sony Music had not settled as of mid-2026; a July 2026 summary-judgment hearing was pending after audio fingerprinting revealed copyrighted recordings in Suno's training data

sources.title: Udio Pricing — Official Page
claims_supported: Udio offers three tiers: Free (100 credits/month, 10 credits/day cap), Standard ($10/month or $8/month annually, 2,400 credits), Pro ($30/month or $24/month annually, 6,000 credits)
Add-on credit packs available: 100 credits for $3, 1,000 credits for $25; purchased credits do not expire
Annual billing saves 20%

sources.title: Udio 2026 Terms of Service: Commercial Use, Ownership & Rights Checklist
claims_supported: As of June 15, 2026, Udio's terms page was last revised November 12, 2025

Udio's terms include restrictive provisions: Udio and/or its licensors own generated works; users may only use them for personal/non-commercial purposes unless the current plan and process permit commercial use
Commercial rights for paid tiers are present but complex — users must verify plan, process, and scope before publishing

After Udio's October 2025 UMG settlement, Udio disabled all user downloads and stems, becoming a streaming-only 'walled garden'; a fully licensed version with re-enabled downloads was expected later in 2026 but had not shipped as of June 2026

Udio's commercial use rights are particularly murky post-settlement; Suno Pro grants clearer commercial rights and downloads at $10/month, while Udio reserves commercial use for its $30/month Pro tier — and even then downloads are disabled

sources.title: iZotope Ozone 12 Advanced — Official Product Page
claims_supported: iZotope Ozone 12 Advanced is priced at $499 (full price); loyalty/upgrade pricing available to existing iZotope customers
Ozone 12 comes in three tiers: Elements (entry-level, AI-led), Standard (14 modules), and Advanced (20 modules, full AI workflow suite)
Ozone 12 Advanced includes AI-powered Master Assistant with Custom flow, new modules: Bass Control, Stem EQ, and Unlimiter
Subscription access also available (reported ~$19.99/month via iZotope/NI subscription)
Ozone is a DAW plugin (AAX, AU, VST3), not a standalone cloud mastering service; it requires the user to work inside a DAW

sources.title: LANDR Mastering — Official Site
claims_supported: LANDR offers three paid subscription tiers: Essentials, Standard, and Pro
All three paid tiers offer unlimited MP3 online mastering; Standard and Pro also include WAV mastering and the LANDR Mastering Plugin
LANDR's free tier in 2026 is preview-only: unlimited uploads and browser previews, but every download requires payment
Entry paid pricing approximately $12.99/month (Essentials, unlimited MP3) to $24.99/month (Pro, unlimited WAV/HD WAV)
Free-tier trial outputs are watermarked with an audible noise burst, making them unusable for distribution

sources.title: ElevenLabs Terms of Service (non-EEA) + Music Terms
claims_supported: ElevenLabs free plan users may only use outputs for non-commercial purposes; commercial use requires a paid plan (Starter $5/month and above)
Paid plan users receive a commercial license for generated audio, including music/vocal outputs, retained perpetually for already-generated content even after cancellation
ElevenLabs explicitly has a Music product ('Eleven Music') with its own Music Terms; free-plan Music users are prohibited from distributing on Spotify, Apple Music, Amazon Music, and Pandora

Paid Music plans (Starter, Creator, Pro, Scale, Business) prohibit use in offline TV/radio/film, live broadcasts with large audiences, and music libraries of 100+ outputs intended for third-party licensing
ElevenLabs prohibits cloning public figures' voices without consent and has suspended accounts for violations

ElevenLabs retains a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free license to use uploaded voice data to train and improve models, but commits not to commercialize a user's voice on a standalone basis without permission

sources.title: AI Voice Cloning Legal Status — US and EU (2026)
sources.publisher: GrowthStackKit / Chartlex (corroborated by multiple independent legal commentary sources)
claims_supported: There is no single US federal law banning AI voice cloning outright as of 2026; the NO FAKES Act has been introduced but not yet passed; the TAKE IT DOWN Act targets nonconsensual intimate deepfakes, not voice cloning generally

US voice cloning is governed by a patchwork of state right-of-publicity laws; Tennessee's ELVIS Act was the first to explicitly name AI voice replicas; at least 12 states have passed similar legislation
Cloning someone else's voice without permission is actionable under US right-of-publicity law and/or applicable state statutes

In the EU, a person's voice is personal data under GDPR, requiring a lawful basis (typically consent) to clone; the EU AI Act (fully applicable August 2026) adds obligations to label AI-generated audio and treat synthetic voice generation as high-risk in mass-media/political contexts
ElevenLabs and similar platforms shift legal responsibility to the user: users must own or have explicit consent from the voice owner before cloning

sources.title: Distributors That Accept AI Music: 6 Yes, 2 No (2026)
claims_supported: DistroKid accepts AI-generated music with mandatory disclosure at upload; no cap on number of AI tracks; does not apply different pipeline treatment to disclosed AI tracks; runs automated AI detection — undisclosed AI content is flagged for review or removed

TuneCore has explicitly stated a policy of not accepting '100% AI-generated tracks' (policy tightened since July 2025); accepts AI-assisted music with significant human creative input; requires attribution disclosure and data licensing assurance
CD Baby rejects fully AI-generated content outright — the strictest policy among major distributors

LANDR (as distributor) accepts AI music but caps uploads at 12 AI-generated songs per calendar month per subscriber, requires disclosure, and excludes AI content from YouTube Content ID, Meta, TikTok, Deezer, Pandora, and Tencent
Six major distributors accept AI-generated music in 2026: DistroKid, RouteNote, UnitedMasters, LANDR, Amuse, and Symphonic

The DDEX AI disclosure standard is being rolled out through distributors (DistroKid, TuneCore parent Believe, CD Baby, Amuse, RouteNote, and Soundrop all signed the Spotify AI transparency initiative as of April 2026)

sources.title: Adobe Project Music GenAI Control — Adobe Research
claims_supported: Adobe Project Music GenAI Control is an early-stage research tool, not a commercially available product as of mid-2026; it was previewed in February 2024 but has not been integrated into any shipping Adobe application
The tool allows text-prompt music generation with fine-grained control over the output (intensity, length, loops, genre)

Adobe has not publicly disclosed the training data used for Project Music GenAI Control; a spokesperson stated Adobe 'has always taken a proactive approach in ensuring we are innovating responsibly' but declined to share model details

A separate, commercially available Adobe generative audio feature — 'Generate Soundtrack' — was announced at Adobe MAX 2025 in public beta, using the Firefly Audio Model to create licensed instrumental tracks synced to video inside Premiere Pro; this is distinct from Project Music GenAI Control

unverifiable_claims: Pricing and feature scope of Melodyne's AI-assisted pitch editing versus manual mode — no search was conducted for Melodyne specifically due to source budget constraints; the writer should verify current pricing at Celemony's official site (celemony.com) before publishing
Current pricing and capabilities of Splice AI tools for sample generation — not searched due to source budget constraints; verify at splice.com directly

Whether Meta's MusicGen or other open-source models are practically usable without coding knowledge — not searched due to source budget constraints; the usability question is also editorial/subjective and depends on user technical level

Training data disclosure status for Suno and Udio — both companies have faced litigation partly over undisclosed training data; Suno's audio fingerprinting revealed copyrighted recordings (per Billboard, cited in independent sources), but neither company has made a formal public training data disclosure; treat any specific claim about disclosed training datasets as unverified for Suno and Udio
Training data disclosure for Adobe Project Music GenAI Control — Adobe explicitly declined to disclose training data details to press (Gizmodo, 2024); no subsequent disclosure found

Whether the NO FAKES Act has actually passed — one source (VoGen) claims it passed in late 2025, but another legal analysis source (GrowthStackKit, updated July 2026) says it 'has been introduced but not passed'; the writer should verify the current legislative status of the NO FAKES Act before publishing any claim about it being law

Exact current LANDR paid plan pricing — official LANDR pricing page returned no parseable price data in the crawl; independent sources cite $9–$39/month range depending on tier, but the writer should confirm current pricing at landr.com/pricing/master before publishing

ElevenLabs current Music product pricing tiers and generation minute limits — the archived v1 Music Terms include limits (e.g., free plan: 11 min/month; Starter: 30 min/month) but these are from an archived/deprecated terms page; current active Music Terms pricing table was not fully retrievable; verify at elevenlabs.io/music-terms before publishing
